data.title: Wall Street ends mixed; Nvidia lifts Nasdaq
data.description:
Wall Street

Wall Street stocks ended mixed on Friday, with Nvidiaclimbing and Microsoft dipping, while Treasury yields declined a day after U.S. President Donald Trump unveiled reciprocal tariff plans but stopped short of imposing new ones.

Yields across government bonds slipped for a second straight day after data showed U.S. retail sales fell more than expected in January, dropping 0.9% last month after an upwardly revised 0.7% increase in December.

The yield on the 10-year note fell about 7 basis points, last at 4.44%.

The Nasdaq 100 which is made up of the Nasdaq exchange's most valuable companies, rose 0.4% and notched a record-high close.

Nvidia added 2.6%, while Apple moved 1.3% higher. Microsoft dipped about 0.5% and Amazon slid 0.7%.

Trump tasked his economics team on Thursday to devise plans for reciprocal tariffs on every country taxing U.S. imports, although the directive stopped short of imposing fresh tariffs.

Howard Lutnick, Trump's pick for commerce secretary, said the administration would address affected countries individually and said studies of the issue would be completed by April 1.

The imposition of tariffs on steel and aluminum imports, a bigger-than-expected rise in January's consumer prices and hawkish comments from Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell added to market volatility this week.

"It's all about Trump right now. All the other stuff is just noise," said Dennis Dick, a trader at Triple D Trading in Ontario, Canada. "What everyone is focused on is, 'What is Trump going to do next, and where are his tariff wars going?'"

Stocks received a boost earlier this week after data showed U.S. producer prices increased in January, while key elements in the core Personal Consumption Expenditures index, a measure closely tracked by the Fed, were benign or lower.

Traders are pricing in at least one 25 basis-point interest rate cut by the end of the year, with a roughly 50% chance of an additional cut, according to LSEG data.

The S&P 500 edged down 0.01% to end the session at 6,114.63 points.

The Nasdaq gained 0.41% to 20,026.77 points, while the Dow Jones Industrial Average declined 0.37% to 44,546.08 points.

Of the 11 S&P 500 sector indexes, seven declined, led lower by consumer staples, down 1.16%, followed by a 1.11% loss in healthcare.

Volume on U.S. exchanges was relatively light, with 14.4 billion shares traded, compared with an average of 15.0 billion shares over the previous 20 sessions.
For the week, the S&P 500 rose 1.5%, while the Nasdaq gained 2.6% and the Dow added 0.5%.

Most of the 11 S&P 500 sectors rallied this week.

Airbnb jumped 14% after the vacation home rentals company posted higher quarterly revenue.

DaVita dropped 11% after the dialysis firm projected annual profit below estimates. Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway also sold some of its shares in the company.

Applied Materials fell 8% after the chipmaking equipment maker forecast second-quarter revenue below estimates.

U.S. markets will be closed on Monday for the Presidents Day holiday.

Declining stocks outnumbered rising ones within the S&P 500 by a 1.3-to-one ratio.

The S&P 500 posted 37 new highs and 7 new lows; the Nasdaq recorded 131 new highs and 83 new lows.
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: Daily Voice: Emkay's Kashyap Javeri likes these 4 themes despite recent correction
data.description:
Kashyap Javeri is the Fund Manager & Head of Research at Emkay Investment Managers

Even without the recent market correction, "what we liked was discretionary spending, capex, CDMO plays and private financiers (bank or nonbank)," said Kashyap Javeri, Fund Manager and Head of Research at Emkay Investment Managers, in an interview with Moneycontrol.

Further, with FII selling facing fatigue at some point and valuations correcting, he believes the opportunities are weighing in favour of investors.

After the recent repo rate cut, according to him, the RBI should incrementally get much more legroom to cut rates anyway if food prices remain under control.

Do you see more headwinds than tailwinds for equity markets for the rest of the calendar year?

With recent corrections in broader valuations, the markets must spend increasing efforts on what are the tailwinds available now. For one, the recent budget announcements on tax cuts and increase in KCC (Kisan Credit Card) limits can give a solid consumption boost. Second, the RBI has already thrown in the towel and cut the rates with gates open for further action down the line. Third, given good growth in HH (household) deposits and lower growth in personal loans, net financial HH savings should improve dramatically versus a low of 5.3 percent of GDP in FY23. Fourth, rural consumption should get a boost from robust kharif and rabi crops. Fifth, if consumption increases, the capex cycle on the private side should get incremental legs to grow.

On the flip side, there is still a sword hanging over the whole world about Trump's tariff tantrums. Secondly, the interest rates in the US economy are refusing to come down due to the same. Thirdly, we have our issues of how to raise employment opportunities in India.

But overall, with FII selling facing fatigue at some point in time and valuations correcting, the opportunities are weighing in favour of investors.

Do you see the possibility of another 5-6% correction from here?

Nothing is impossible. But having said that every such market correction has done two things for sure. One, bring a new flock of investors to the market and two create a 2-3 year window for making 15-20% compounded returns.

Do you see the tariff risk easing in the after part of the year?

London's weather and Trump's mood can change any time. They are as unpredictable as they can be. He was supposed to give a Valentine's day gift on the 13th to the world in the form of a reciprocal tariff but suddenly it whimpered down to just the formation of a new committee to study the tariff patterns. So we don't know how it will turn out.

Having said that it is also quite possible that the sword is being used to bring people to the negotiating table and it may turn out to be just nothing.

Which sectors are looking interesting for investment during the current correction?

Even without recent correction what we liked were discretionary spending plays, capex plays, CDMO plays and private financiers (bank or nonbank). Nothing changes.

Do you think the RBI will need to deliver more than a 50 bps repo rate cut (in addition to the 25 bps cut in February) in 2025 to boost the slowing economy?

The RBI should incrementally get much more legroom to cut the rate anyways if food prices remain under control. The core inflation has been under or around 4% for quite some time. If with great kharif/Rabi production and good sowing of vegetables, food inflation remains under control, RBI will follow up with more rate cuts.

Even as of today, the RBI is doing as much and more to boost liquidity with VRR (variable rate repo) and OMOs (open market operations).

Are you becoming more bullish on chemical stocks after the Q3 earnings, and do you expect better growth in Q4 and beyond?

Yes, incrementally specialty chemicals should do well.

Disclaimer: The views and investment tips expressed by investment experts on Moneycontrol.com are their own and not those of the website or its management. Moneycontrol.com advises users to check with certified experts before taking any investment decisions.

data.title: India PE investors double down on seeking alpha amidst shifting valuations
data.description:
India PE Investors Double Down on Seeking Alpha Amidst Shifting Valuations

India's private equity landscape is dynamic, with recent headlines highlighting both the immense potential and the challenges of the market. The ongoing discussions surrounding the valuation markdowns of some prominent late-stage startups, coupled with the continued fundraising success of certain growth-focused funds, underscore the need for a nuanced approach to investing in this space.

One of the core themes is the debate about public vs. private markets. While public markets have historically been the focus for many investors, a growing number of sophisticated players are increasingly looking towards private assets, despite the acknowledged illiquidity and longer-term nature of these investments. This shift raises important questions about the drivers behind this trend and the types of investors leading the charge.

Against this backdrop, the PMS AIF World – a new age alternative investment focused wealth platform for HNIs, led a panel of discussion on strategic capitalization of early-to-mid stage private equity opportunities for better wealth creation.

Putting forward his reasoning behind the current investment climate, Ashish Ahluwalia, Director for Private Equity at Axis AMC, said "Smart investors recognize the value creation happening in new and disruptive industries, much of which occurs before companies go public." First, they recognize that "they are seeing new industries being formed and they are seeing traditional businesses being disrupted by new businesses," and these new businesses are "creating tremendous value for their investors."

Looking at the past decade of IPOs, he noted that "some very large businesses have come out which have come out of the private equity and venture capital," demonstrating the value creation potential. Second, these investors understand that "in a lot of these industries a lot of the value is already discovered by the time they come into the market." While value creation certainly continues post-IPO, "smarter investors will say that a lot of the value is getting discovered by the time they come into listing." Therefore, the desire to participate in this value creation emerges at an earlier stage (stage n-1) to capture higher returns.

Third, allocation plays a significant role. Unlike public markets, where a diverse portfolio can be easily constructed, private companies are different. Ashish pointed out that "typically if you see when these companies are coming to market I am seeing a good company a typical small cap would be raising 500-600 crores of money on the IPO." Even when these IPOs are oversubscribed, "an investor who is trying to get an allocation in this through a listed mutual fund a small cap or a micro-cap mutual fund that mutual fund typically ends up getting 10-15 crores of allocation in that company."

This leads investors to seek "a vehicle where I get a meaningful allocation," which again points to "stage n-1." In short, "smarter investors have understood...that there is disproportionate value being created by new businesses and they need to enter the private markets." This trend, he added, is visible in "sophisticated financial markets across the world – US, Europe and later China – which is you need to then start allocating a bit of your money to private markets to get this disproportionate share of new value."

Herein, the most promising sectors as per Amit Ratanpal, Founder and Managing Director of Blink Investment Management, are education and financial services sectors because tThese are fundamental sectors of the Indian economy. Almost between 25 to 35% of the overall private equity allocation goes to financial services and education," he said. He pointed out the sheer scale of the opportunity in the education sector with "about almost 150-175 billion children going to the schools," yet "the gross enrollment ratio is less than about 30%, while the developer market is more than 65%.

“You have to be patient in this sector” and be led by a long-term view, he suggested. While acknowledging the influx of funding into the education sector in recent years, he observed that almost 80% of the funding went into only two or three centres that is K-12, or tutoring services within the K-12. This leaves behind other crucial areas like "early childhood education [and] skill development" underserved and ripe with opportunity.

Turning to financial services, Ratanpal described it as "always [being] the backbone of the economy." He sees increasing robustness due to "the digital public goods which the government has been creating whether we UPI or the unified lending interface. With about 63 million MSMEs and the overall penetration at just about 15%, there’s still immense space to catch up for the investors, he highlighted. Besides, there is also low penetration rates for credit cards and mutual funds/depository services. "Now India becoming a 10 trillion dollar economy and then moving to a 20 trillion dollar economy, lot of asset and wealth management creation is going to happen."

While the potential for high returns in early-to-mid stage companies remains attractive, this potential is inextricably linked to the risks involved. A critical component of mitigating those risks lies in robust corporate governance. Navin Honagudi, Managing Partner of Elevate Venture Partners, elaborated on the red flags he sees in this process. "One key thing we look to avoid is investing in 'party routes' – companies with complex ownership structures, often involving forward-driven investments.

Data shows these often lead to lower returns, even if the underlying business is good, because valuations become inflated," he explained. He also stressed the importance of founder alignment, stating, "I strongly believe founders should take some money off the table at various rounds... However, there needs to be a limit. 1-2% of their shareholding per round is reasonable. Excessive selling is a red flag."

"Another red flag is when founders are not aligned with the long-term vision and are more focused on short-term gains," he said while pointing to the current market debate of overhyped valuations. "A lack of profitability, particularly when coupled with aggressive growth targets, can be a major warning sign," he said. "Sometimes for founders to taste their own success...there has to be a limit to it,” he suggested with 1-2% of their shareholding at every round being optimal.

From a governance perspective, a strong board committee as per the industry is important. Adding to this, he believes that trying and beating their own projections as close to possible is another positive sign not only in case of a public company, even in a private company. Even though early-stage projections can be challenging, but as companies mature, “there should be a reasonable merit towards running in the right set of projections".

Ahluwalia drew some conclusions at the end of the panel discussion, with a conversation on the trends in the current space. He noted good incidence of capital going back to investors both foreign and domestic in the last few years, and increasing sophistication and participation of domestic investors. The challenge remains with the tech sector as it r continues to face the "funding winter".

A key focus of Ashish's analysis was valuations, believing that the current environment presents a compelling opportunity, particularly for late-stage growth investors. “What's happening to valuations on the public markets, we're seeing that tickle down into the private markets” he said. While acknowledging that private market valuations don't perfectly mirror public market fluctuations, he sees a growing "pricing sensibility" emerging. This trend is likely to continue as companies "going for IPO is just getting higher." He believes that India's ambition to become a $10 trillion economy hinges on significant private sector participation.
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: Wipro ropes in Accenture's Amit Kumar to head its consulting division
data.description:
Wipro

Software major Wipro on February 14 announced the appointment of Amit Kumar as Managing Partner and Global Head of Wipro Consulting, effective immediately.

"Amit will report to CEO and MD Srini Pallia and join the Wipro Executive Board," the Bengaluru-headquartered company said in a statement.

The appointment comes at a time when Wipro has been highlighting that the consulting business, led by its subsidiary Capco, has been doing well in the past couple of quarters. "Capco, has grown 11% year-on-year. Bookings are up 9% year-on-year," Chief Financial Officer Aparna Iyer told analysts on January 17, 2025, after declaring the third quarter results.

Wipro is also doubling down on inorganic expansion in the space, announcing on December 16 the acquisition of Massachusetts-headquartered IT services consulting firm Applied Value Technologies (AVT) for $40 million to augment its existing application services capabilities.

Prior to joining Wipro, Kumar served as the Managing Director for Accenture Consulting, where his roles included the Americas Market and Industry X Consulting over the last 17 years.

Kumar will lead Wipro’s efforts to drive consulting-led, Artificial Intelligence-powered growth, and guide clients through business and technology changes. He will leverage Wipro’s expertise to deliver transformative AI, data, and industry solutions, helping businesses scale and grow amid digital disruption.

“His (Kumar's) experience in driving exponential business growth, combined with his passion for innovation and results-driven approach, makes him the ideal leader to propel our consulting business into an exciting new future and deliver superior value to our clients,” Pallia was quoted as saying in the release.

With over 24 years of consulting experience across North America, Europe, and the Asia Pacific, Kumar brings expertise in industries such as High-Tech, Chemicals, Energy, Retail, Automotive, and Consumer Goods. He brings expertise in Business Transformation, Operational Excellence, Engineering, Manufacturing Digitization, and Supply Chain Management.

Kumar is a graduate of St. Stephen’s College, Delhi, and holds a master’s degree in
management from the Symbiosis Institute of Business Management, Pune. He will be based
in the greater New York area.

Glance at Leadership

At Wipro’s latest earnings press conference, Chief Human Resources Officer Saurabh Govil said it’s "business as usual" when asked whether senior leadership exits had stabilised.

The company has stepped up its efforts in developing leadership. The company expects Wipro Leadership Institute to train over 600 leaders by FY25. This would be through a combination of in-house leadership and programs curated with leading global institutes.

Additionally, Pallia highlighted that the company will not hesitate to go to the market and get leaders. "We will continue on that journey," Pallia had said in January.

Meanwhile, the senior leadership exits seem to have stemmed now, with the company poised for growth.

On November 25, Wipro appointed Omkar Nisal as its new Europe Chief Executive Officer, succeeding Pierre Bruno. Bruno was the fourth big departure from the Wipro stable after Pallia took charge on April 6 from Thierry Delaporte.

While Chief Technology Officer Subha Tatavarti resigned on August 12, Chief Operating Officer Amit Choudhary and Asia Pacific, India, Middle East, and Africa (APMEA) president Anis Chenchah resigned in May.

Interestingly, Choudhary, Chenchah, and Delaporte have all worked in France-headquartered IT firm Capgemini. Bruno, Tatavarti, Choudhary, and Chenchah were appointed during Delaporte's tenure at Wipro.
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: SME Action: Tejas Cargo India, Royalarc Electrodes IPOs remain undersubscribed on Day 1
data.description:
IPO News

The initial public offerings of Tejas Cargo India, and Royalarc Electrodes could not attract healthy demand on their first day of bidding as both remain undersubscribed on February 14.

The subscription will remain open for both public issues till February 18, followed by the process of finalisation of IPO share allotment on February 19.

Tejas Cargo India IPO

The logistics company targets to raise Rs 105.84 crore via initial share sale of 63 lakh shares. The price band for the book-built issue has been fixed at Rs 160-168 per share.

Investors have bought 13.18 lakh shares till Friday, which were 29 percent of its offer size of 45.24 lakh shares, via 1,082 applications, the subscription data published on the NSE showed.

Click Here To Read All IPO News

On February 13, the Faridabad-based supply chain transportation services provider has already raised Rs 29.82 crore by allocating 17.75 lakh shares to 8 anchor investors at a price of Rs 168 per share.

Tejas Cargo is going to utilise IPO proceeds for the purchase of additional trailers, working capital requirements, and the repayment of debt.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

New Berry Capitals is the sole book running lead manager handling the public issue.

Royalarc Electrodes IPO

The initial public offering of Royalarc Electrodes was subscribed just 12 percent on Friday, with investors buying 2.65 lakh shares against the offer size of 21.6 lakh shares via 214 applications.

The welding consumables manufacturer entered capital markets to mobilise Rs 36 crore via public issue of 30 lakh shares which comprises of fresh issue of 18 lakh shares and an offer-for-sale of 12 lakh shares.

Out of which, it has already raised Rs 10.08 crore via anchor book on February 13. Four institutional investors picked 8.4 lakh shares in the company via anchor book.

The company that makes welding electrodes, flux cored wire, and MIG/TIG wires will utilise fresh issue proceeds for the expansion of the manufacturing facility at Valsad (Gujarat), working capital requirements, and general corporate purposes.

Fedex Securities is acting as the merchant banker for the issue.
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: Umesh Agarwal of 360 ONE shares his filters for stock selection; should be a unique player and double earnings in 3 years
data.description:
representative image

Umesh Agarwal, fund manager at 360 ONE Asset has outlined his key filters for selecting stocks, focusing on companies poised for an initial public offering (IPO) within the next seven months to four years. His investment strategy targets businesses in the post-Draft Red Herring Prospectus (DRHP) stage as well as late-stage companies with strong growth potential.

Agarwal in an event organized by PMS AIF World said that he invests in companies that are expanding faster than their respective industries, operating in large markets with substantial Total Addressable Market (TAM), and capable of doubling earnings within three years. He expects revenue growth of 15–20% from such companies.

Another filter he considers is a limited peer set. He believes that when a company goes public, it should stand out in its sector rather than compete with numerous similar firms. "When it eventually goes public, there shouldn’t be dozens of similar companies in the market, as investors love unique businesses," he says.

Pricing discipline also plays a major role in his selection process. "While issuers and bankers often push for valuations based on public market comparisons, Agarwal maintains that private companies should be valued with an illiquidity discount," he says. However, he says that pricing has become more reasonable in recent times.

PMS AIF World is a modern, alternates focused investment platform designed specifically for High Net-Worth Individuals (HNIs) and prioritizes wealth creation through alpha-driven investment strategies.

Disclaimer: The views and investment tips expressed by investment experts on Moneycontrol.com are their own and not those of the website or its management. Moneycontrol.com advises users to check with certified experts before taking any investment decisions.

data.title: Private credit emerging as alternative in hybrid and debt market, experts say
data.description:
“While residential real estate is simple and relatable, commercial real estate offers rental income and appreciation. Globally, rental assets are owned by institutions like insurance companies and REITs. India’s REIT market is still nascent, but new-age AMCs are offering off-rent yielding strategies that provide returns of 15-18%,” he explained.

The private credit market in India is witnessing a significant growth. As a part of panel during PMS AIF World's Crystal Grazing 6.0 event, experts noted that the private credit space was picking up and was here to stay.

PMS AIF World is a modern, alternates focused investment platform designed specifically for High Net-Worth Individuals (HNIs) and prioritises wealth creation through alpha-driven investment strategies.

Nachiket Naik, Head - Structured Credit, Alternates by Axis AMC explained that historically, credit markets in India have been bank-enabled. However, he added that bond markets primarily cater to high-investment grade assets – the AAA and AA spaces. "With banks and NBFCs moving towards small-ticket lending, a gap has emerged in the non-bank credit space. That is where private capital in a closed-ended format becomes a logical answer. This has driven the upsurge in private credit platforms,” he added.

According to Naik, the space is still evolving but is here to stay. “Globally, debt is bigger than equity. In India, we are just warming up,” he added.

Rohit Rathi, Principal - Real Estate Business, ICICI Prudential AMC noted that there has been increasing investor participation in real estate. He added that real estate is the largest asset class in the world and an asset class that is very close to everybody's heart.

“While residential real estate is simple and relatable, commercial real estate offers rental income and appreciation. Globally, rental assets are owned by institutions like insurance companies and REITs. India’s REIT market is still nascent, but new-age AMCs are offering off-rent yielding strategies that provide returns of 15-18%,” he explained. Rathi added that currently we are at a peak interest rate cycle, hence any reduction will positively impact commercial real estate. “There are now multiple opportunities for investors to participate,” he said.

Dipen Ruparelia, Head of Products, Vivriti Asset Management noted that traditional fixed-income products are offering negligible post-tax, post-inflation returns. On the other hand, "Private credit provides an alternative with predictable returns,” he added.

He also noted that private credit can also play an important role in reducing portfolio risk. “Unlike equity, which is subject to large volatility drawdowns, private credit offers steady returns. Most funds target 14-15 percent gross returns, making it an important component of any debt asset allocation strategy,” he explained.

Disclaimer: The views and investment tips expressed by investment experts on Moneycontrol.com are their own and not those of the website or its management. Moneycontrol.com advises users to check with certified experts before taking any investment decisions.
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: Banking's big moment: Vikas Khemani, Sunil Singhania bullish on the sector amid changing market trends
data.description:
Given the weak sentiment, the Nifty Bank index has slipped over 2 percent this week.

As global markets recalibrate to the evolving macroeconomic landscape, seasoned investors Vikas Khemani and Sunil Singhania see a bright outlook for Indian banking, IT, and manufacturing. While their optimism is anchored in structural trends, they caution that stock performance often moves independently of sector fundamentals.

Khemani, Founder of Carnelian Asset Management & Advisors notes a significant influx of foreign debt flows into India over the past week, signalling growing investor confidence. "This tells us that investors are not worried about country risk," he said at the PMS AIF World Conference on February 14. PMS AIF World is a modern, alternate-focused investment platform designed specifically for High Net-Worth Individuals (HNIs) and prioritizes wealth creation through alpha-driven investment strategies.

Also read: Where does US run deficits and whom will reciprocal tariffs hurt more?

He also highlighted how improving liquidity conditions and policy pivots are setting the stage for market expansion. He remains particularly bullish on banking and IT, citing strong demand in India and the US as tailwinds for technology firms.

Sunil Singhania, Founder of Abakkus Asset Manager, broadly agreed with Khemani and offered a nuanced take. "Sectors may perform well, but stock prices don’t always follow suit," he observes, pointing to banking’s stellar 2024 performance in terms of asset quality and profitability—yet lacklustre stock returns. Conversely, IT, despite its weakest profit growth since 2008, saw a sharp stock market rally.

For banking, however, Singhania sees an inflection point. "We believe global inflation and interest rates are peaking," he states. If rates trend lower, liquidity should improve, and the Reserve Bank of India may adopt a more balanced approach to economic growth. Additionally, banks are trading at significant discounts to their historical price-to-earnings (PE) multiples, with even large banks below 15 times earnings, making them attractive.

Read more: Over 100 countries secure from reciprocal tariffs as they run trade deficit with US

Manufacturing, another bright spot, carries a valuation conundrum, Singhania adds. "Three months ago, valuations were pricing in the sky; now, they’ve come slightly lower but remain at a premium," Singhania explains. Profit growth must surprise on the upside for further re-rating. He remains cautious on government-dependent sectors like defence and railways, where a single buyer—India’s government—can dictate fortunes. "We saw how defence stocks tumbled 10-20 percent after a disappointing budget allocation."

Pharma remains a sector of interest, but trade tensions loom large. "If the US imposes tariffs on pharma imports, it could be disruptive. But I doubt they can afford to cut off generics—it would spike their costs and create shortages," Singhania reasons.

As markets adjust to shifting global trends, banking emerges as a clear beneficiary. "Whether this plays out remains to be seen," Singhania concedes, but it’s a compelling case.

Nifty Bank index closed at 49,099, lower by half a percent. Given the weak sentiment, the Nifty Bank index has slipped over 2 percent this week.

Disclaimer: The views and investment tips expressed by investment experts on Moneycontrol.com are their own and not those of the website or its management. Moneycontrol.com advises users to check with certified experts before taking any investment decisions.

data.title: Voler Car IPO subscribed 12.38 times on final day; P S Raj Steels, Maxvolt Energy public issues close with decent subscription numbers
data.description:
IPO News

Employees transportation services provider Voler Car's initial share sale was subscribed 12.38 times on the final day of bidding, i.e. February 14, while P S Raj Steels, and Maxvolt Energy Industries closed their IPOs with single-digit subscription numbers.

All these three companies will finalise their IPO share allotment by February 17, while the trading in their equity shares will commence on the NSE Emerge effective February 19.

Voler Car IPO

The company that provides home-to-office transportation for employees of large MNCs and corporate entered capital markets on February 12 to raise Rs 27 crore via initial public offering. The price band for the offer was Rs 85-90 per share.

Investors have applied for 2.68 crore equity shares against the offer size of 21.66 lakh shares via 9,052 applications, the subscription data published on the NSE showed.

Click Here To Read All IPO News

Voler Car, which is valued at little more than Rs 100 crore, intends to utilise IPO proceeds for its working capital requirements, and general corporate purposes.

P S Raj Steels IPO

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

The initial share sale of the stainless steel pipes and tubes manufacturer saw 9.2 times subscription during February 12-14, with investors picking 1.33 crore equity shares against the offer size of 14.51 lakh shares via 7,013 applications.

The Hisar-based company approached capital markets to mobilise Rs 28.28 crore via maiden public issue at the upper end of price band of Rs 132-140 per share.

These fresh issue funds will be utilised mainly for its working capital requirements.

Maxvolt Energy Industries IPO

The lithium-ion batteries manufacturer also managed to attract decent demand for its IPO which was subscribed 3.07 times on Friday. Investors have applied for 65.92 lakh shares against the offer size of 21.48 lakh shares through 2,649 applications.

The company that manufactures lithium-ion batteries under the MaxVolt Energy brand for electric vehicles, energy storage, and electronic launched its Rs 54-crore public issue on February 12. The IPO was a mix of fresh issuance of 24 lakh shares worth Rs 43.2 crore, and an offer-for-sale of 6 lakh shares worth Rs 10.8 crore.

Maxvolt Energy Industries is going to utilise fresh issue proceeds for repaying its debt, purchase of plant and machineries, and general corporate purposes.
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: Bulk deals: PGIM India sells 0.77 percent stake in Concord Enviro Systems
data.description:
Representational Image

The following bulk deals were recorded on both exchanges on February 14.

Concord Enviro Systems
PGIM India Equity Growth Opportunities Fund Series I sold 1.60 lakh shares (0.77 percent stake) at an average price of Rs 529.9 per share. Concord Enviro Systems shares were locked in lower circuit of 20 percent at Rs 523.05. This came after the company reported a net loss of Rs 8.56 crore for the quarter ended December 2024 against net profit of Rs 18.58 crore during the previous fiscal.  The Mumbai-based company provides water and wastewater treatment solutions.

Moksh Ornaments 
Vincent Commercial Company Limited bought 6.15 lakh shares (0.71 percent stake) of the company at a price of Rs 12.94 per share, while Pine Oak Global Fund sold 6.14 lakh shares (0.71 percent shares) at an average price of Rs 12.94 per share. Shares for the company closed at Rs 13, around 0.85 percent higher. The stock has fallen around 23 percent over the last one year. The Mumbai-based company manufactures & wholesales gold jewellery.
